data.title: You Can Beat The Market - By Breaking Some Rules
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

David Gardner, co-founder of The Motley Fool, author of Rule Breaker Investing, shares why it's possible to beat the market (0:30). Investing is not just a math exercise (5:00). Starbucks stock and playing the long game (8:20). Investing principles that have led to wins (12:40). Management is key (19:00). Overvalued as a buy signal (26:20). Parallels to dotcom era (33:45).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome to the show David Gardner, co-founder of The Motley Fool, author of the fantastic new book, Rule Breaker Investing. David, so happy to have you on Seeking Alpha. So happy to have you on Investing Experts. Thanks for coming on.

David Gardner: Well, thank you very much for the invitation, Rena. I'm really looking forward to the conversation.

RS: Likewise. You know, The Motley Fool and Seeking Alpha both really at the forefront of helping retail investors, of helping investors secure the best alpha, secure the best profit.

Also, we are in the midst, as everyone seems to be aware of, of many, many paradigm shifts happening in the world at large, in the economy, in the stock market.

You just wrote a book, Rule Breaker Investing. What would you say right now as investors try and understand this perhaps new iteration of the markets, what is best to keep in mind these days, would you say?

DG: Well, I think first of all, I've always loved your name Seeking Alpha. I also like Rena. That's actually my daughter-in-law's name. But I was gonna call out Seeking Alpha because I think that's what we should be doing as investors.

I really believe that it is not just not luck to beat the market. I believe it's very possible to beat the market, and I believe that I have done that over thirty years now.

And so the purpose of Rule Breaker Investing is to basically leave it all out there on the field. This is my last stock market book, and I try to put into it everything that I use to beat the market.

To answer your question, I think the first thing upfront is the time frame that we're gonna take to our investing. I feel very strongly that you should have a whole life time frame, not a not a three year time frame, not a ten year time frame.

I think you should be invested your whole life and just keep buying and dollar cost averaging into the best individual stocks you can find.

Of course, for years of The Motley Fool, we've said index funds are great, and they are for a lot of Americans who just don't wanna spend time listening to us on a podcast.

Index funds are a wonderful answer. But I suspect because of the audience we have listening to us today, they're interested in beating the market. And for me, the best way to do that is to find excellence, buy excellence, and add to excellence over time and sell mediocrity, and time is the key player.

So those are a few I realized, yeah, there are some paradigm resets and other things happening in society. The oncoming AI reminds me of the dawn of the Internet. There's a lot of analogies I see thirty years apart between these things.

And it was always the right move thirty years ago to be playing the long game as an investor and buying great stocks, and that's gonna be true over the next thirty years as well.

RS: Do you also believe that part of the portfolio is meant to be played with - less about more certain picks towards excellence and more guesses at excellence?

DG: I really appreciate that question. I would say, my principle number one of the rule breaker portfolio right there at the start of part three is make your portfolio reflect your best vision for our future.

So as you might imagine, I'm a huge fan of people making their portfolios fun for them, personal. I feel like I should be able to look up and down your brokerage statement, and I should know you better because the companies that you're invested in should really say a lot about you in the same way our books say a lot about us.

What's on our shelf says a lot about what's in our brain. I feel very strongly the same should be true of your your portfolio. So, yeah, I think it's fine to have some speculative aspects. I think we should always be learning, always pushing out the boundaries a little bit more.

I would keep almost any stock that I bought, I don't think I'm right. I'm not ever positive. I don't go in with this high confidence. That's why I really believe you should start with a portfolio of 20 stocks at a minimum, and then let's see what horses start getting ahead down the track and then invest accordingly.

And one thing I love about the stock market more so than horse betting is you can bet all the way through the race. So as Secretariat starts surging out front, you can bet on Secretariat then. You don't have to just guess ahead of time. So I think it's very helpful to know the things and observe the world that you're investing in. But I also believe that it's helpful to always be trying to learn a little bit more.

So five to 10% of a portfolio could be merely speculative, and it shouldn't just be too crazy. I think it should be to learn and have fun.

RS: Not enough Secretariat references these days, David, really appreciate that.

DG: Let's go.

RS: Let's go. I'm curious. You know, as a co-founder of Motley Fool, as a writer, what have been some of your rules that you've had to break along the way? What has been most salient in that journey for you?

DG: I think first of all, as an investor, I started with the idea that investing was mainly a math exercise. And I was raised to enjoy math. I was actually an English literature major, but I've always loved baseball statistics, and I love sports and stats. So I'm very numerical.

And my dad who taught me investing, taught me how to value line. That big black tome with tons of numbers. It's still around as a business. It's not as big as it once was because the internet has shown up in the meantime.

But I had to disabuse myself of all of the mathematical habits that I'd gotten into thinking that that's the way to invest. And of course, that's important. But the qualitative side of investing is I think really where I buttered my bread as an investor a lot in my book, Rule Breaker Investing, is about the qualitative things, the things where there are no numbers.

One of my key lines in the book is there are no numbers for the things that matter most. And, for example, who the CEO of the company is matters hugely to your investment returns. There is no line item anywhere in the financial statements for the plus or minus value of that person.

So it's like watching the NFL trying to guess what's gonna be a good team, but not being allowed to know what the quarterbacks are of any of the teams. That's how people are investing if they're making it pure math without looking at the qualitative inputs that determine the outputs of cash flow and earnings.

So that would be a a really good example, I think, for me as a younger investor. You know, starting The Motley Fool, we had a lot of people in my twenties and thirties relying on the advice that my brother Tom and I were putting out through our website. People paying us for our next stock pick.

And so you learn in an accelerated fashion. You need to learn what's gonna win and work when you have a lot of people following you. So that was an incredibly helpful lesson. And I've learned a lot more lessons.

I'll just stir one more thing in the cauldron here, as an entrepreneur myself. And so you know, Rena, working within a business and I know you've been at Seeking Alpha for around seventeen years. You know that you get such insights working within a business yourself and then you can translate those into the stock picks and analysis that you do of other people's companies, which is what we do when we pick stocks.

So my favorite Warren Buffett line and I have very mixed feelings about Warren Buffett. I I'm a huge fan of his character, but I have a very different approach to beating the market.

But one of my favorite lines from Buffett is, I'm a better investor because I'm a businessman and a better businessman because I'm an investor.

And I really believe those things are integrally connected. And I didn't understand that when I first started investing in stocks at the age of 18.

RS: Yeah. And I would say I did not understand that seventeen years ago as I came on board at Seeking Alpha at the dawn of the great financial crisis. There was a lot to learn about the world and how intertwined everything is, especially in the business world and in the financial world and in the stock market world.

You mentioned the story at the outset of your book about going on The View and trying to bring in stock investing to the masses and how that can be a difficult errand because of people's desire for quick returns, instant gratification. It's hard to play the long game in investing.

I'm curious what has strengthened your conviction, or what has helped you encourage investors to strengthen their conviction? And I think part and parcel of what we've been talking about so far is the the importance of having your own strategy work for you. You have to be okay with whatever your strategy is. Maybe kind of if you can stir all that in the same cauldron and and and I'm interested to hear what you have to say about that.

DG: Well, first of all, just about The View. We picked a stock in 1998, and then we were invited back six weeks later just to update the story on the ABC daytime show. And the stock had lost 33% of its value in just six weeks, which obviously was a surprise to us. And they were good natured about it, but we were never invited back - not a year later or, let's say, twenty seven years later to update the story.

The stock that we picked for the view viewers was Starbucks (SBUX). Starbucks had a bad few weeks that summer when CEO Howard Schultz basically underpromised and would later over deliver future earnings estimates. And so that dumped the stock in the six weeks since we picked it on ABC.

Starbucks is up 34 times in value from the day we first appeared on on ABC. So it's a reminder, obviously, what game are we playing. And I would say it's actually not that hard to play the long game.

I think a lot of people watching us right now, especially people who've been active as investors their whole lives or are in investment clubs, a lot of us, I think, can appreciate and I hope have a long term mentality around our money.

I do think it's hard to do on TV. So I don't think CNBC, I don't spend any time watching CNBC. I think so much media coverage of our markets forces a short term viewpoint. And while ABC's The View is not a stock market investing show, it's sort of a television show.

That's why I've always loved the Internet because the Internet has a memory and you can score things, tag, date stamp things, and check back later. And so I've always been an Internet junkie. But your question was also about conviction and how you develop that and how you grow that over time.

And I think, first of all, as a fool, I never go in... I always come from a place of humility. So as I mentioned earlier, any stock that I recommend or buy, I'm saying I think I'm right. But I don't ever know I'm right. And in fact, one of my, key habits as an investor, as a rule breaker investor is I only add to to winning positions.

I never have added to a losing position. And I realize a lot of people who would call themselves value investors or big Buffett fans might think that's crazy because they're like, hey, if you liked it at 27, surely you like it at 17.

And my answer is not for the kinds of stocks that I recommend that I found over thirty years. Often, they don't really come back in a reliable way.

So I've grown conviction around my style of investing, but we can easily see somebody else who would grow conviction as that stock drops from 27 to 17 that it's an even better buy. And certainly for some companies, that style works.

I think part of it is, what color is your parachute? What is your style of investing? And I think part of the challenge of life is know thyself. That's why it was scrawled on the Greek oracle at Delphi, know thyself.

We're all trying to figure ourselves out over the course of our lives, and we try to get better at that. And as you do, you should let that determine how you invest your money.

So I have a very specific brand of investing that I've sort of self styled and built over thirty years. Happy to say I've found some of the great stocks you could have found, and I bought them early, and I'm still holding them over those thirty years. So that's part and parcel to my strategy, but I realized there are many others, and I hope I did a decent job speaking to that.

RS: I think so. If you could speak a little in more granular detail about your strategy because to your point about when to buy and when to sell and the price, and valuation is such a question mark these days.

It's like, what price is at what stock and why? And is it divorced completely from reality? And so I'm curious to know the metrics that you're basing your strategy on and and various factors like that.

DG: You bet. Well, I have six traits that I look for in stocks and specifically in rule breaker stocks. The more check boxes I can give to the six traits from a single company, the happier I am to to invest in it.

And I'll just run through them really fast because and if we wanna dip into some of them, we can. But here they are in order.

Trade number one is top dog and first mover in an important emerging industry. That always has me focused on the innovators, that has me focused on the upstarts, that has me focused on the Davids that are trying to take down the Goliaths.

And that's why I've managed to find Amazon.com (AMZN) at my cost basis, thanks to stock splits, which is now 16¢, which I've held since 1997. And it also had me recommending, to Motley Fool members, NVIDIA (NVDA) in 2005. Finding it early, still holding it now twenty years later. And my cost basis, thanks to stock splits, is coincidentally 16¢.

So both of those companies are iconically great companies and have ended up being, I think, the best stocks of this era, and they both conform to that first trait of rule breaker investing.

They also conform to the other five. Here they come. Number two, we're looking for companies with sustainable competitive advantage that can be measured in different ways. Some of it is left brained, some of it is right brained.

We can talk more about that. But because I invest for at least three years, sustainable, competitive advantage is critical to my style of investing. Number three, this is the first one that's not about the company. It's about the stock.

Trait number three, this one's contrary. Stellar past price appreciation. I want the stock already to have done well. In a world where people are trying to buy low, sell high, they're waiting for dips, I'm doing the opposite.

We can talk more about that. So those are the first three. The second three, numbers four, five, and six. Number four is good management and smart backing.

The more I've lived, the more I realize the importance of who's running the company. As it turns out, Jeff Bezos had a cost basis of 16¢ while he looked young and seemed like a crazy man. Barron's said amazon.bomb in 2001. His value to Amazon and to investors and Amazon was incalculably high.

And that's really important to recognize. So I'm a huge fan. I'm basically a humanist, small h, because I really think you need to find greatness in people and be invested there in a world where many people don't even care who the CEO is. They're looking at zigs and zags or stock analytics, stock charts. So that's number four.

Number five, strong consumer appeal. I'm a huge fan of branding and great brands. I think those are great moats, competitive advantages under themselves, but I also love the brand isn't measured in the financial statements.

And so every great company looks overvalued because it has a great brand, and no one's counting that in their, price to earnings ratios.

And then the final attribute, the final trait we're looking for in our six traits of the rule breaker stock is that the company be broadly perceived as overvalued. And we can talk more about why I actually think that works even though you'd think it would be the opposite of what you'd wanna do.

So those six traits, something I love about them are they're obviously a main feature of this book. But I first wrote them down in our 1999 Motley Fool book, Rule Breakers, Rule Makers.

The difference between me then and me now is that then I was in my early thirties saying, here's what I think works. This is what I'm doing. I'm putting it out there. And now here I am twenty seven years later going, I'm still using the same six and they have worked wonderfully.

And I believe they will work in every era. And I think people should know these, not for every stock. Because these don't really speak to cyclicals. These are never gonna speak to meme stocks, which I have no interest in.

And they don't speak to lots of other types of companies on the market. After all, how many companies truly are top dog and first mover in an important emerging industry?

The answer is all the best ones. And even the ones that don't work out, I'm fine with because I have more of a VC mentality where as a venture capitalist, I'm willing to lose.

But I'm not a VC at series a or angel investor. I'm a VC for public market stocks, and I hold them for long periods of time.

RS: There's a few things that I would love to pick out. First, I'm curious, on one foot, how many stocks would you say fits under that rubric?

DG: It's a great question. In a lot of ways, the question is how many industries are there? And I tend not to really care about traditional industry designations.

So if I see someone screening for stocks and they've got tech stocks as one of their their industries, that's a phrase that's never meant anything to me.

My first appearances on CNBC in my twenties, I was like, what is a tech stock? Could somebody please define what that is for me? It's never had any meaning to me.

RS: I would say it's gotten less meaningful as the years have gotten.

DG: Yeah. And I appreciate that; I agree with that.

So how we define what an industry is itself sort of a creative choice. And therefore, I don't know that there's a single number we could put on it, but I would say that, I would be open like, generally, the stocked pond of the fish, each of which is a top fish and first mover in an important emerging industry, probably comes down to somewhere between two hundred and five hundred stocks, which may sound like more than people would expect, except that I think that how we classify industries is very interesting, and we can be more interesting in our classification.

So that's roughly how many stocks I'd be looking at.

RS: I would say the sectors is another thing that we could say is shifting its paradigm for sure all over the place.

The other thing I was curious about, we mentioned Starbucks. You mentioned Amazon, two great examples of managers to value and and be impressed by.

In terms of that as a factor, something you're looking at, is there something we had, an analyst, Jack Bowman, on a couple months ago talking about how Amazon - most people think that its moneymaker is Prime, but really it's AWS and Prime is a money loser.

Are there steps along the way or choices along the way or choices not made along the way where you're like, wait a second. He has great background. He has great history. He's done a great job other places. But I'm really questioning or is that the point where you rely on the other factors to kind of bolster your case?

DG: A fun question. And I will just say, first of all, there's no way to nail the answer to that question because it's sort of open ended.

And that's that would be my point is that, part of what I love about caring deeply about who's running the company is we can never really fully know these people.

I mean, even somebody like Elon Musk who is about as overexposed as any business leader in history, he's still largely a mystery to many people. With that said, I've never been an Elon fanboy, but I'm definitely not an Elon hater.

And it seems like the world is divided into those two groups. I'm right in the middle. Actually, I would say I lean toward fanboy because I think there have been few greater value creators, I won't just say living today, I might even say in business history when you look at the scale at which he's achieved innovation and business gains across many different disciplines or industries.

So I think that's a great example. But, you know, does he have a background that would allow him to really run Neuralink or really know what he's doing with brain computer interfaces, I don't exactly know.

But that we're asking that question shows that we're barking up the right tree, I think, because these are the questions that will lead to the actual great winning stocks or great losing stocks of the next generation.

Many people think it's all about waiting for a dip to hit their valuation model for a certain company. And while I certainly respect valuation, they're very short term, the valuations people do.

And as soon as the company morphs, like AWS shows up, anybody's valuation model from five or ten years before is rendered irrelevant and so I don't think it's worth spending a lot of time there.

I generally think that the market is pretty efficient in the near term. It's pricing in all that it can.

But I think the key to beating the market is if you just think more than six months ahead, which is what I personally believe the market factors in, it doesn't factor in six years for sure.

So it's much shorter term. So if you just think a little bit longer than the average dollar out there, I think you put yourself in rarefied air. There aren't a lot of other people flying around up there trying to beat the market, and yet there's where most of the market beaters live.

So I think, again, part of this is you're asking a beautiful question there. Like, what can we know about this person?

How could you have known that Howard Schultz tried to extend Starbucks at one point into a music brand. It was gonna be like a destination music site. That was a a bad chapter in Starbucks' history.

Reed Hastings, maybe my favorite living CEO (NFLX) simply because he's made me more money as an investor than any other CEO. We may remember Qwikster. I think the year was 2011. He decided to split the DVD rental legacy business from the streaming oncoming business. And as a customer, I was like, wait. What? You know, why do that?

And he rebranded the DVD rental business Qwikster, and the stock lost two thirds of its value inside of a year. I just kept holding because I believed in Reed Hastings, but even, really, even the best make mistakes.

So I think that in the end, it's about the observation that you and I give. It's not about any firm knowledge that this person has it nailed in every market era.

RS: And being quick to be resilient and accountable for missteps, would you say?

DG: Yeah. I totally agree. And, obviously, Reed Hastings would be a great example of that.

I also think we need to be resilient because I think, Rena, a lot of people sell emotionally, especially in the face of, you know, Netflix loses 30% of its value, then it goes down 50%, then it's down 66%.

There are a lot of people at a certain point, they're like, I'm out. This is bad. But it really wasn't bad.

And part of what the stock market does, and I try to write about this in Rule Breaker Investing, is it overshoots constantly either direction because it's guessing, and God love it. I love the stock market, and I'm glad that we're all looking ahead trying to figure out what it all means.

But the reality is that business results themselves are much stabler. I'm making up these numbers. You can fact check me, and I'll have it a little bit wrong. But during that period where Netflix lost two thirds of its value in one year because of Qwikster, I think their customer base declined from, like, 25,000,000 subscribers to 24,000,000 subscribers.

That 4% loss of subscribers dumped the stock more than half of its value. And that's because, in part, it was richly priced in the first place.

People were thinking it was going for 25 to 35, and all of a sudden, it's going 25 to 24 because of a self inflicted gunshot wound. And that's why you and I, why we need to show resilience and go, you know what? They can get over this. The business isn't actually harmed that badly.

I've seen this a number of times behind my rule breaker companies because usually they always treat trade at rich valuations.

And so anything that goes wrong, NVIDIA is gonna lose as it did just a few years ago more than half its value. Even at the breathtaking size it is today, that kind of volatility really throws people.

But I'm not gonna say they're all weak hands, but there are enough weak hands out there that you and I really benefit when we just stay in it and keep adding.

RS: Yeah. And I think preventing yourself from making emotional rash decisions is one of the best things you can prevent as an investor. And if you can't, that's a really good time or to find a good person to get yourself a teacher or somebody that's gonna help you.

Because if you can't help yourself find somebody who can, there's a lot of people that can.

DG: Well said. And, obviously, the whole investment asset management industry is there for you. That's why it exists because people decide they either don't want to or, as you just mentioned, can't. They wouldn't do as well as giving it over to professionals.

Of course, part of the spirit of Motley Fool, and I suspect Seeking Alpha as well, but I won't speak for you, is to serve those individual investors who do wanna do it themselves.

I love that base of people because in my mind, those are the intellectually curious people in our society. They're often entrepreneurs themselves. This is the group of people I wanna hang out with at gatherings of our customers and members.

And so we're not serving usually the people who have the weekends, who don't wanna do it themselves, or just totally divorce themselves from their 401k. We're all about the people who are trying to do better.

RS: First of all, love an intellectually curious person. Absolutely. And, also, I think there are shades of independent investing, in terms of who you rely on, how many people you rely on, and what percentage you rely on.

To your point about NVIDIA and valuation, and you spark the conversation of enjoying something that is being overvalued, which we're really in the thick of these days. Maybe speak a little bit more on that point.

DG: Sure. I think this is maybe my favorite chapter in the book. It's chapter 12. Every chapter of my book is like 10 or 15 pages. It's designed to be a fast, fun read. Of course, if you're a fool, you need to make people laugh at different points. So I hope it's really enjoyable to read.

RS: It really is a good read. It really is a good, enjoyable, fast read.

DG: Thank you, Rena. Since it's my final stock market book, I want it to be a good one going out.

But, I think chapter 12, I address something that intuitively I knew and was working so well for me over 30 years, but I couldn't figure out why it worked. And you and I have already touched on it lightly.

It's me saying the sixth trait of the rule breaker stock is that it be overvalued, broadly perceived to be called out on CNBC or Barron's or The Wall Street Journal or even Seeking Alpha or even The Motley Fool.

People are saying that stock is, quote, so overvalued, end quote. And since I watched this as a young investor, I first saw it with AOL, which was my first great stock, and it was constantly being called way overvalued, and it was my best stock pick.

It went up a 150 times in value over the six years that, we held it. And then it eventually merged with Time Warner, and then it didn't really end well from that point forward.

But as a lesson, unbelievably helpful for me. I was like, okay. So I think it's because the other five traits are top dog and first mover in an important emerging industry, sustainable advantage, excellent past price appreciation, good management, smart backing, and strong consumer appeal.

That's hard to do, by the way. Any company that has those five traits is probably pretty awesome. And then if people are then calling out as totally overvalued, these are the best companies of our time.

And so in chapter 12, I'm like, okay. So why why does this work? And I believe at the heart of it is this. There are no numbers for the things that matter most.

So what you have is you have a world that is valuing stocks off of earnings or cash flow multiples or book value in some cases. And what they're not ever looking at are we've already touched on a few of these.

Who the CEO is, the company's brands. There might be a goodwill value if that company has acquired another company and there are intangibles above the tangibles that can't be accounted for.

They call it goodwill. And sometimes they'll say that's the brand, but there's no real goodwill value like that for Apple (AAPL) or Starbucks, some of the great brands in the world. So there's no number for those on the financial statements.

There's two other things that I think about that have no numbers that matter at least as much as those two. Can the company innovate or not? All the great companies can innovate their way out of problems, and all the bad companies can't innovate their way out of a box.

And so the innovative capability of companies, you know, what Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) slash Alphabet does versus those that try to compete with it like Yahoo, which basically Google ran out of business over the course of time, would be a good example.

So the innovative capability probably in this topsy-turvy world you and I are living in, that we're all living in, where AI is coming, and there are all kinds of changes happening, who can innovate? Who can morph into the next butterfly? That is incredibly valuable. There is no number on the financial statements for that.

And then the final one I might mention is just corporate culture. This is something I care about deeply. I'm happy to say at The Motley Fool, we've had some award winning moments for our corporate culture over the years. I know having started something as a paper newsletter from my den thirty two years ago.

I know how important culture is for companies, universities, organizations of all kinds. Culture runs deep. In fact, culture usually lasts longer than leadership. You can toss your leader, but that culture doesn't change overnight.

Cities are built on the bones of cultures and themselves breathe out culture. So culture matters deeply. There are no numbers expressing the value or lack of value of corporate cultures.

So I think I've just hit on maybe the four most important things of business, which I've seen as an entrepreneur and as an investor. Who's running it? The brand name or reputation, can it innovate, and the company culture. And there are no numbers on the financial statement.

So you have a world full of people who are saying it's 71 times earnings. That is crazy overvalued, which is what Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) was when I recommended the DaVinci Surgical Robot Maker in 2004.

And it looked like it was overvalued at 71 time until I started realizing, actually, that's a great buy signal because everyone else thinks this is overvalued. But I think this company is the dawn of a sea change from physicians cutting us apart with scalpels to minimally invasive robotic assisted surgery, which by the way, I think all surgery is gonna be before you and I are done.

And so there are no numbers to express those things, and yet it's qualitative and it's these are the best stocks of our time.

NVIDIA, Amazon, Tesla (TSLA), Intuitive Surgical, the list goes on. And I've heard it since I picked each one of them from day one. They're all so overvalued. Palantir (PLTR), most overvalued stock of its time. The list goes on.

And I started to realize around thirty years ago, this is actually an incredibly great buy signal that is being handed to those of us hidden in plain sight that wanna pay attention, not to every company.

There are lots of overvalued meme stocks and a lot of junk stocks that I would never buy. But for these types of companies, that is a buy signal. I hope that makes sense.

RS: Yeah. Absolutely. And and I think to your point, about the designation of what the tech sector is and that AI will probably be in the entire health care sector and what that means and how we understand things.

Curious, what awards did Motley win for company culture?

DG: Glassdoor, best company to work for best small to medium sized company to work for, but this is more like 2015. Actually, we won it two years in a row, which is not easy to do when there's, like, 10,000 businesses out there.

Then COVID happened and then we went remote. And I would say our culture is different. It's probably not award winning today because we're trying to figure out how to be awesome in this new world.

Another example of change in the world today, but those are formal recognitions. I mean, I would say that we've always been a conscious capitalism, fun company, best best place to work for kind of a thing.

I'm sure you guys are too. I would say especially for white collar digital businesses, it's a lot easier to be a great company to work for than a much more industrial, tougher kinds of business.

So sometimes it's easier to win awards like that. But we're definitely proud of our corporate culture at The Fool. But this isn't about ours. This is about us being anthropologists looking into the cultures of any company that we're thinking about buying, especially if we're gonna hold the stock for at least three years, maybe three decades.

RS: I was thinking that maybe my last question to you would be something like you live through the dotcom phase, and how does that parallel what we're in now vis a vis the AI boom, etcetera.

But, if you feel like there's better value elsewhere that you have to share, happy to hear your final words or words of encouragement that investors should keep in mind these days.

DG: Sure. Well, first of all, your questions have been outstanding, and you told me offline before we started, the purpose of your work here is to maximize the value with each guest. So I hope I've contributed all that I can, and I think your questions have done that. And keep up the great work. So thank you for that.

I think what I wanna speak to is AI because a lot of people have it in their minds right now. And maybe a word of encouragement because often we're hearing that AI is gonna put you, me, all of us out of our jobs. And in some cases, that already is true, and in other cases, it will be true.

And yet what happens throughout history is that older jobs get replaced by newer jobs that are just flat out better jobs than what you and I were doing before.

And, obviously, there's a transition I watched that happen with the Internet. A whole bunch of jobs went away. Newspapers changed, needed to become digital, etcetera, as one example.

Yet, I think it's been replaced consistently from those who first started banging rocks together a few thousand years ago. As we've innovated, we've gotten better and better jobs and callings. So that's encouragement.

But I wanna close with specific encouragement for investors because there's also some people saying, you know, you don't need to pick stocks anymore, or AI is just gonna do all the investing for us. And, obviously, in a lot of ways, AI has been doing the investing for us.

In this sense, a lot of trading in funds that people put their money on is algorithmic, and computers are driving program trading and have for decades. And, therefore, for those of us who aim to beat the market, who are trying to retire early or go for outsized returns, I actually don't think AI is a particular threat. It might be a help, but more than anything, for those of us in that position, I think it's kind of neutral to good.

And here's what I mean. If you have beaten the market over the last ten, twenty, thirty years, you were all along competing against AI.

As soon as somebody invented a computer and started getting popular forty years ago, others were programming those computers to try to beat the market and make money for free all day, all the time.

So that has been on both sides of many trades for decades now.

Really, since we started The Motley Fool in the mid nineties and the Internet came on, people have been using program trading most of the volume today, on Wall Street, the Nasdaq, then NYSE today.

Most of that volume is computer driven algorithmic trading, which has happened for years and years. So I don't believe there's some new force that's gonna enter the market and take away all the alpha.

In fact, most people who use AI are trying to make money instantly inside of a second. That would be high frequency trading. That's not being done by humans and hasn't been for the longest time. So if you're somebody who believes in playing the long game, I don't think there's much competition left for you.

And in fact, it's gonna take years for AI to have enough data for really long time frame periods to to know with confidence what maybe you or I should do.

But I think increasingly, it'll be proven out that the more patient you are with your money, the more likely you are to do better.

Brokerage firms always report that their inactive passive accounts are outperforming their their active traded accounts. And, I think all of those truths are eternal verities. And I believe you will continue to be rewarded by playing the long game and looking for excellence.

And if that's what I'm doing, and I'm loving this market. And I don't care if the market drops next year because one year in every three, the stock market does drop. And that's part of investing your whole life long.

I'm gonna have many more drops in future at the age of 59 just like we've had in the past. You started your work during the great financial recession, your personal work at at Seeking Alpha. You know exactly what I mean. That's gonna happen again.

Maybe in three years or sixteen years, I'm invested all the way through buying and holding the best of our time. And that's what Rule Breaker Investing teaches. That's what we're about.

RS: Well, thanks, David. There's so much to digest and think about here. The book again is Rule Breaker Investing. Thank you for being so kind. Thank you for being so generous. Thank you for sharing so much. This is like conscious investing also. It's like really what you're touting here. You're welcome back anytime.

DG: Thank you, Rena. Keep up the great work. We appreciate you.

RS: Appreciate it. Appreciate you right back.

data.title: Waste Connection: Solid Cash Flows In Waste Management's Best-Run Compounder
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Waste Connections (NYSE:WCN) offers investors a glimpse into one of the sturdier corners of the environmental services universe, and it does so from a foundation of balance-sheet solidity and operating consistency. Its underlying municipal collection base offers centers of stability, while higher-margin niches, such as E&P waste and intermodal and special waste disposal, provide vectors of expansion and expansion of margin. That combination has yielded recurring, visible cash flows, with the company itself converting more than 90% of EBITDA into operating cash flow, a percentage that separated it even within a strong-credit peer group.

What is exceptional in WCN is not rapid growth but discipline and compounding. The company grows mainly through tuck-in acquisitions in secondary markets to achieve pricing power and avoid the high capital intensity and competitive overlaps imposed on larger peers. That construct keeps return on capital high and cash generation stable to support dividend growth and buybacks and still cover network growth. Effectively, WCN gets downcycle resilience and exposure to secular tailwinds in the waste-management space such as increased regulation, landfill shortages and innovation in the recyclables space.

At ~17× forward EV/EBITDA, WCN is priced in-line with sector multiples widely but with five-year averages ~18× EV/EBITDA and around 50× P/E, typical of a historical scarcity premium. With both EV/EBITVA and P/FCFPS frameworks suggesting 20-40% upside to FY27, the current configuration leaves room in the coming few years for slow appreciation even on conservative estimates. The dividend yield is low at ~0.7%, but alongside consistent cash-flow growth, the total return case strengthens significantly.

And so I vote bullish: WCN needs no rerating tale or Brave New World growth forecasts. It already boasts one of the cleanest balance sheets, converts cash best of peers and has shown disciplined acquisitions and secondary-market density to steadily compound value. That, in my view, is one of the cash-reliable and resilient ways to be long environmental services in the medium term.

About the Company

Waste Connections (WCN) is North America's third-largest integrated solid waste services company operating in the United States and Canada within a secondary and exclusive market-based model. The company has a solid waste collection and disposal and energy & production (E&P) waste treatment and recycling business. Its complement is an intermodal and specialty waste business.

Its competitive advantage is built on density-driven economics and local market exclusivity. About 40% of revenue is derived from exclusive or quasi-exclusive markets providing recurring cash flows and less vulnerability to competitive pricing pressure. At the same time, WCN funds its core through a disciplined roll-up strategy: dozens of tuck-in acquisitions annually, expanding its geographic coverage and enhancing route density

Recent Performance

WCN announced a solid Q2, highlighting the strength of its secondary-market business. Revenue reached $2.29 billion, up ~7% y/y, with organically rising by ~2% in equal parts by price (+6.6%) and lower volume (-3.5%). Solid waste collection remained the principal source of growth, while intermodal and E&P waste showed a recovery on sturdier U.S. shale basin activity.

Margins held their ground in the face of inflation pressures. Adjusted EBITDA hit $733 million, or a ~33% margin, up 70 bps q/q on the back of pricing discipline, recycle benefits, and cost leverage. Net income was ~$290 million ($1.12 per diluted share), up from $1.07 in the preceding year, and free cash flow hit $367 million to preserve >45% EBITDA conversion.

Management pegged full-year guidance higher, and FY25 revenue now guides at $9.45-$9.65 billion and adjusted EBITDA at ~$3.12-$3.2 billion (33%-33.3% adj EBITDA margin). Interesting to note is FCF conversion remains within the 48%-50% bracket, allowing considerable room to maneuver on both M&A and shareholder returns.

Topline and Margin Outlook:

In my view, Waste Connections is still a defensive compounder with density and price on routes leading the earnings machine, specialty waste and RNG providing optionality for expansion, and free-cash-flow conversion supporting shareholder payout. Let's take a look at a segment view for each:

Solid Waste Collection “steadily grows, pricing first”

Collection is WCN's leading source of recurring/high-visibility revenue engine. Economics are simple: thin local market competition & high-density routes imply that pricing is stickier than headline volume. In Q2, volumes reduced while pricing increased 6-7%. Management commentary is that price coverage is essentially intact to a broad extent throughout the year such that good visibility into H2 margins is present. Looking through to FY27, collection revenue should be into mid-single-digit growth corridor primarily through disciplined yield to 4-5% p.a. & moderate volume growth reflecting broader population & industrial activity about ~1% growth. Margin profile should be good on the other side because incremental price falls directly to EBITDA once routes & associated fixed costs are fully loaded. Additionally, operational improvements & recurring small tuck-in M&A should also bear fruit to generate low-single-digit cumulative margin expansion to FY27.

Solid Waste Management and Transfer "volume-sensitive but structurally defended"

Transfer and disposal make up physical heart of the system and thus align with industrial activity flows as well as seasonality. Economics of landfill (long useful life spans, amortized capex with controlled gate revenues) give WCN a margin floor, and transfer scale and capacity enable it to capture marginal route volumes. From FY25-FY27, I would expect disposal revenues would align low to mid-single-digit growth and be coupled with overall economic cycle as well as discrete centers of region-specific demand. The margins would be consistent with moderate pricing as well as ongoing optimization of transfer and hauls together with transfer station turns; ongoing upside would be captured from additional routing efficiency as well as selective gate price resets.

Solid waste recycling volatility today, medium-term structural rebound

Recycling is most sensitive to commodities within a portfolio. Q2 showed squeezed recycled commodity prices can pinch near-term cash flows even with flat volume. WCN's exposures have been mitigated through blended revenue streams (service fees + capture of commodities), better downstream contract structures, and capex across sorting/processing to lift realized yield. My take is that for FY26-FY27, revenues should be flattish across the group level (with region deviations), but margins should increase steadily with commodities reverting to norms and with contributions from recent capex spends & contract repricings. Recycling is still a near-term volatility tamer but an upside options generator as markets & realization trends turn better.

E&P Waste Treatment, Recovery and Disposal "cyclical, directional upside if oil activity recovers"

E&P waste is cyclical and follows upstream drilling, completions, and commodity price flows. Lower volumes in some basins were noted on Q2 call but also confirmed WCN's redeployment capability to seek higher-margin treatment and recovery services where demand is strong. Into FY26–FY27, I see a sequential recovery in E&P spend enabling low-to-mid-single-digit revenue growth with operating leverage to the upside as higher-margin treatment and recovery spend contributes. This segment contributes to WCN's optionality: upstream spend is gaining momentum, it's an important incremental EBITDA generator; decelerating upstream spend, the balance portfolio supports the core.

Intermodal & other "small today, strategically accretive" projects

Intermodal, special services, and other ancillary lines are currently a small percentage of the group but have long-term strategic value (logistics, transfer, and commercial connections). I expect Revenue growth here will be ahead of the group average to FY26-FY27 (high double digits possible) as WCN rolls services into bundled routes and transfer footprint, and the contribution to margin is highly accretive on a dollars-per basis since these activities exploit existing infrastructure and sales connections.

All in "modest margin upside, durable cash flow"

Taking all arguments together, the logical forward trajectory for group revenue in my view is to be in the mid to modest high single-digit CAGR range through FY27, with collection and Energy-like specialty services mitigating declines in recycling and the bumpy E&P patch. On the margin side, guidance puts the group at ~33% adjusted EBITDA in 2025; with pricing carryout, additional operational improvement, and a normalization of the commodity environment, I look for gradual expansion through the 33-34.5% range by FY27. Free cash flow should be robust (management guided in the vicinity of $1.3bn adj. FCF in 2025), and it should be levered to support buybacks and accretive tuck-in acquisitions that drive further margin expansion.

Financials

Peer Comparison & Valuation

Among North American landfill operators, WCN is at the high end of peer multiples but remains attractive when one factors in growth, margin strength, and cash conversion. Relative to EV/EBITDA (~17.2× forward), the company is at a premium to (WM) (14.7×) and (RSG) (15.9×), however the premium is warranted by higher gross margins (~42% compared to 40-43% at WM/RSG) and the best cash conversion ratio in the group (CFO/EBITDA at ~93%, ahead of WM at 80% and at RSG at 81%). That is to say WCN is best at translating accounting profit into cash and by the capital intensive nature of the industry, investors should pay.

Through the lens of P/E, WCN's valuation is fair and not stretched. The stock trades on ~27.9× FY27 earnings in a moderate premium to RSG (~27.2×) and above WM (~22×). The main observation is that the multiple is significantly below its five-year historical average forward P/E of nearly 50×, highlighting that the market is already pricing in a more conservative attitude. With earnings growth running at ~15% CAGR through FY27 in lockstep with WM but ahead of peers other than RSG, the implied PEG of ~1.9× appears fair. For comparison, a similar multiple is applied to WM but at a lower ~1.5× PEG reflecting a softer incremental margin leverage and less penetration in high-value niches like E&P waste and intermodal. RSG trades on a comparable multiple but with a lower ~11% EPS CAGR. Against that background, WCN’s premium is justified through strong cash conversion (~93% of EBITDA to CFO compared to ~80% at WM and RSG) and visibility into free cash flow per share growth to drive long-term valuation support as well through the lens of P/FCFPS.

Trading on a P/FCFPS basis below their company-specific five-year mean (current implied FY26/27 22-26× versus historical 33×), with FCF/share compounding from $5.4 in FY25e to $7.7 in FY27e, valuation differential closes organically and facilitates upside even if multiples re-rate sharply.

Our intrinsic valuation framework points to this room. Applying the five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple (18×) would translate into equity value per share up from $183 in FY25 to $233 by FY27 or ~33% capital upside. For a check and balance using a P/FCFPS approach, the same story emerges: equity value per share of $179 in FY25 to $254 in FY27 or ~45% upside. Adding the ~0.7% dividend yield brings the total return potential into the high 30s-mid 40s over a three-year period.

All in, WCN is no "cheap turnaround" but a compounder holding steadfastly below historical multiples despite industry-leading cash conversion and margins. Compared to peers, the premium is warranted; compared to history, it provides valuation space. That dual foundation sector premium, historical discount, bodes well for further upside.

Risks

Critical WCN risks include macro and regulatory factors and less execution-specific to the company. A greater-than-expected decline in construction and industrial units could reduce special waste and intermodal segments' waste volume and could impact revenues and earnings. Unexpectedly higher inflation in fuel or labor costs could squeeze the margins unless wholly offset by pricing action. Further, any unexpected regulatory changes in landfill facilities and requirements on recycling may introduce associated compliance costs. Finally, valuation sports a premium relative to the vast majority of comparables by our estimates, so a miss in growth or cash flows could be proportionately penalized. Despite that, WCN’s robust recurring exposure to municipal collection, contractual pass-through characteristics, and best-in-class cash conversion provide significant insulation and keep downside risk in check.

Conclusion

Waste Connections boasts one of North America’s best-balanced North American environmental service profiles: a stable core of municipal collection, high-margin special waste, and cautious capital allocation. Trading at ~17× forward EV/EBITDA and ~28× P/E, shares are pricey in absolute terms but compared to historical averages and free cash flow opportunity is valuation room. EV/EBITDA and P/FCFPS frameworks both show 20-40% upside to FY27 and are supported by modest dividends. Risks around macro volumes and regulation exist but WCN’s consistently strong cash conversion (CFO/EBITDA ~93%) and stable municipal exposure mitigate downside risk. It is in my view neither a turnaround nor a rerating story but story of compounding: investors are paying fair multiple for durable cash flows and still have room to benefit from gradual multiple normalization and free cash flow expansion.

data.title: Nasdaq: Fairly Valued For Steady Compounding
data.description:
Nasdaq, Inc. (NASDAQ:NDAQ) is certainly not a new name to readers of Seeking Alpha. Despite the leading role of their exchange in the stock market, it's easy to forget that they are a candidate for investment sometimes.

I don't think the business has any troubles, and NDAQ's main problem is its entry price, so I've initiated coverage with a Hold rating.

Business Model

Most people know that NASDAQ is a major exchange and might think there isn't much more to it, but Nasdaq (the business) is like a table with three legs, having three key sources of revenue:

  • Capital Access Platforms ("CAP")
  • Financial Technology ("FT")
  • Market Services ("MS")

CAP and MS most directly tie into the concept of an exchange, monetizing the issuer side and the investor side of it. CAP's revenue sources include:

  • Listing fees from issuers who list their security on NASDAQ
  • Software for issuers, such as blueprints for Investor Relations pages
  • Index licenses, such as for the NASDAQ 100 Index (NDX) and many others

MS is on the investor side, and this revenue comes from using the exchange to trade. Customers here are generally broker-dealers, market makers, institutional investors, and even other exchanges.

FT is their more subtle segment. The leading products here are their crime management and regulatory software services. These assist financial institutions who may lack the means to develop in-house resources for tracking financial crimes or regulatory reporting. Nasdaq's size and deep understanding of these things allow them to create a product for customers who may not even be on the issuer or investor side of exchange business.

While the simple answer is that Nasdaq is an exchange, CEO Adena Friedman declared during Q4 2024 earnings: "we are a software product business," and I agree that this is the right way to view the company.

Financial History

The last decade of financial results can give insights into why Friedman feels this way.

The history of net revenue shows consistent growth, despite the fact that market cycles often impact exchanges.

Above, I've extracted the revenue breakdown of the company (before rebates and other transaction costs) over many years. I indicated the MS portion, which used to be a larger portion of revenue overall. While some of the old segments have since been combined, it is clear that their software products, like those in FT, have been a major source of growth in more recent years. MS even showed signs of cyclicality in later years, as markets became more bearish.

The chart above shows that net revenue growth has resulted in growth of free cash flow as traditionally calculated. I've also shown an adjusted figure, which takes into account the impact of M&A and stock-based compensation. This is because M&A has been very common, occurring every year between 2015 and 2023. The CFO also clarified in the Q4 2024 earnings call that buybacks are done, in part, to offset dilution from stock-based compensation. I treat this as, effectively, a cash operating expense.

Consistent with all this M&A and emphasis on software products, most of Nasdaq's assets are goodwill and intangible. Meanwhile, they hold about $8.7B in long-term debt, largely incurred over time for these acquisitions.

The latest Form 10Q, however, shows that the maturities are generously staggered for them, several years into the future. With FCF and AFCF typically exceeding $1B, it at least shows that the company did not undertake leveraged acquisitions that didn't offer flexibility in repayment.

Altogether, it's healthy balance sheet for a cash-generative business, successfully implementing a growth strategy that mitigates the cycles that exchanges face.

Outlook and Valuation

In the long term, I am not worried about Nasdaq operationally. If we don't believe their leading exchange is going to make it, for one, we probably wouldn't be spending time on Seeking Alpha either. It's that essential to the world of investing.

We might quibble about some of the growth assumptions in the market. Above, using 2024's FCF figure, the recent share price of $96 suggests that a double-digit CAGR over the long term is on the table. This doesn't take into account the adjustments either. It's easy to understand some of the optimism.

Q2 results show double-digit growth in net revenue, felt across all segments.

Significantly more IPOs and new listings on their exchange occurred compared to the prior-year quarter.

The number of licensed ETPs have grown too, as have total AUM in them.

Unlike trading volume on other exchange-based models, these things are more durable forms of cash flow and not as exposed to cyclicality. This is especially true of their non-exchange software products.

Nevertheless, it's fair to say that there isn't a large, untapped TAM of un-listed stocks and ETPs. These depend on markets generally feeling good, which Nasdaq does not control. Listing and license fees may be sticky through mini-cycles, but plenty of stocks and ETPs can disappear entirely if the companies or their assets fail. The same goes for anything tied to their exchange operations.

In other words, Nasdaq can see revenue contraction on the downside of a major cycle. At the very least, growth can skid to a halt.

How do we manage our risk for this? I think we do it by picking good entry prices for NDAQ.

Above, I've posed a DCF that starts with lower FCF to allow for the adjustments I've made and assumes only a 5% CAGR. Priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of a market index), that would make $36 a fair price. That's a big range between $96 and $36, so it depends on if you want fair value for the optimistic case or would prefer an entry price that allows you to be pleasantly surprised.

I think below $60 is reasonable enough, and there have been such entry points in the last few years. With the current price, I just don't think NDAQ is undervalued.

Conclusion

Nasdaq is a legacy company and has plenty of years left as long as there are capital markets. There's little new business to create in the world of exchanges, however, and this is why growth has been driven by M&A. This is a finite resource, just as there is a ceiling on how many IPOs and ETPs there can be. Recognizing this, I'd prefer not to pay for double-digit growth that may not transpire. In the interest of risk management, I decided to take a step back and rate the stock a Hold.

data.title: Under Armour: No Faith In Near-Term Turnaround Potential
data.description:
Summary

I gave a sell rating to Under Armour (NYSE:UAA) in May, with my key thesis being that the valuation was too high and I did not think it accurately reflected the deteriorating fundamentals. That call turns out to be right, as UAA shares have fallen to $4.80 vs. ~$7 in June. The recent developments update removes any lingering optimism, with revenue declines deepening, tariffs creating a $100 million earnings drag, and guidance reset sharply lower. The turnaround has increasingly turned unlikely, and as such, I remain sell-rated.

The Turnaround Hits a Wall

I was skeptical that a near-term turnaround was realistic given the persistent revenue declines and the growing tariff overhang. My skepticism appears to be right, as these concerns are now officially weighing on UAA’s outlook. Whatever lingering optimism that some bulls may have is now off the table as management has revised guidance downwards, which is much worse than I thought. As a recap, management’s outlook for 2Q26 calls for adj. EBIT of just $30–$40 million, driven by a 6%–7% y/y revenue decline and a sharp 340–360 bps contraction in gross margin. This reset implies EBIT to fall by >50% on a y/y basis (2Q26 vs 2Q25). Even if we look at the full year, the story is no better. Management is guiding for adj. EBIT to be roughly half of FY25 levels, which equates to ~$100 million. I don’t see this as just a slowdown but as a clear affirmation that demand conditions are worsening.

The most damaging factor is the new tariff headwind, which management quantified as a ~$100 million drag to cost of goods sold in FY26. This alone translates to ~200 bps of gross margin pressure for the year. The bigger issue is timing. Mitigation efforts like supplier cost-sharing and selective price increases are not expected to deliver meaningful offsets until FY27 or later, which means UAA is highly exposed for the next twelve months. Speaking about timing, this headwind came at a time when UAA is arguably the most vulnerable. Recall again that UAA is in the process of repositioning the brand towards a full-price strategy (which I highlighted previously as risky), and as I expected, it is not working, as direct-to-consumer sales have evidently weakened without promotions. Now, with tariffs inflating costs, I think management is in a lose-lose dilemma. They either absorb the costs and watch margins collapse or attempt to push through higher prices in a promotional-heavy environment and risk further “brand damage” as more price-sensitive consumers leave the brand. Neither outcome is attractive, and both materially hurt the P&L.

Looking deeper into the operations, nothing positive has really surfaced since I last covered the stock. North America and footwear, which should be the two most important drivers of the turnaround, showed little traction.

Again, as a recap, North America, which is UAA’s largest and most important market, saw a 5.5% y/y revenue decline in 1Q26 and is expected to see a low-double-digit decline in 2Q26. The main problem here is what drove this decline. According to management, it was due to (1) a poor spring/summer 2025 wholesale order book; (2) falling retail traffic; and (3) competition from the e-commerce environment that is hurting its full-price strategy. I have already discussed about (1) before, so I am not really surprised, but rather, I am again disappointed there is no progress.

What is new is the second and third points. For the second point, if it is falling “transactions,” I would not be so worried because consumers are still coming into the store but leaving because they didn’t find what they want (this shows that the UAA brand is still relevant). However, with “traffic” declining, it sends a stronger message that consumers are not resonating with the brand. I may be overthinking here, but if this is as I expected, UAA is going to face a really tough time turning around the business. For the last point regarding competition, this is really bad because it adds another strong layer to the bear thesis that UAA is further unlikely to be successful in its full-price strategy. This is especially true in the current cost-conscious environment, where I expect consumers to gravitate towards e-commerce sites that offer more value products (which also means lower prices and a price ceiling for UAA products).

Even in footwear, things have not turned for the better as well. For reference, revenue in the category dropped 14% y/y in 1Q26. As I’ve argued before, footwear is the key to any performance brand, as it drives credibility, relevance, and consumer engagement across all categories. While the decision to sunset the Infinite running franchise may be the right call strategically (since it is underperforming), as it allows UAA to reallocate resources to new products like its recently launched Halo, the reality is UAA will experience a major sales vacuum that will hurt the P&L (it takes time for the Halo product to ramp up).

One last data point to share is the quarter-to-date sales, based on the alternative data available. As you can see, they are well in the negative zone and way below consensus. As such, I really don’t see the potential for a turn around anytime soon. Until there is clear evidence of a turnaround in both North America and footwear, I believe the fundamental equity story remains deeply impaired.

Valuation

The debate is when UAA will turn around the business, and my view is there is no visibility to this at all. The developments so far suggest that UAA has yet to find a formula that can work, and while they are in this process, the P&L continues to get hit by deteriorating demand, competition, and cost pressure. Despite this, consensus is already pricing in UAA to turnaround the business (revenue growth expected to turn positive) in FY27. I struggle to find a strong reason this could be the case, based on whatever we are seeing today. From a valuation basis, this implies UAA to trade at 17x 2-year forward PE, which is surprising to me because it is at the +1 std level. For a business that has shown no signs of turnaround, I think the market has turned too optimistic on the timing of recovery, which makes the risk/reward skewed to the downside. If UAA continues to disappoint or guide down again, I see a high chance that valuation will collapse easily back to the mean or below the mean.

Investment Risk

There are upside risks. The strong performance in EMEA, which saw revenue grow 10% in 1Q26, could serve as a successful playbook for other regions, and should it work well, it could potentially accelerate the North American turnaround. UAA also managed to deliver gross margin expansion of 70 bps in 1Q26, which may suggest that early strategic initiatives have some traction. Should the noted green shoots in brand health and improving consumer perception translate into tangible demand sooner than expected, the current negative sales trajectory could reverse, leading to a significant upside surprise.

Conclusion

I maintain my sell rating. With North America weakening, footwear collapsing, and tariffs adding pressure, there is no visibility to recovery. Valuation still embeds overly optimistic expectations for FY27, leaving significant downside if guidance cuts persist. Until there is clear evidence of traction in its core markets and categories, UAA remains, in my view, a broken equity story.

data.title: Dutch Bros: Visibility To EBITDA Growth Has Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Dutch Bros Inc. (NYSE:BROS) previously with a hold rating, as the valuation was already pricing in the upsides even though I like the fundamentals. The story has meaningfully improved since my last write-up. Core catalysts I highlighted, such as digital adoption, food innovation, and stronger marketing, are now delivering tangible results, driving a clear inflection in traffic and transactions. With valuation now more reasonable and visibility to EBITDA growth improving, I believe the setup is compelling enough to upgrade my rating.

The Growth Flywheel Is Gaining Momentum

Apart from valuation coming down (more below), a key reason for my upgrading to buy is because the drivers I previously identified as catalysts (digital adoption and food innovation) are now delivering tangible results. This can be seen in the latest quarterly results that showed a clear inflection point in traffic and transactions. For better reference, I will draw some data from the latest quarterly results: System-wide same-shop sales [SSS] grew 6.1%, supported by a 3.7% lift in transactions, in which company-operated shops' SSS grew 7.8%, supported by a 5.9% increase in traffic. The focus on traffic and transaction datapoints matters because it tells us that growth is being driven by bringing more customers through the door and increasing visit frequency, not just leaning on price, which is far healthier and more sustainable.

On digital adoption, I believe this has contributed significantly to the momentum. For instance, the Mobile Order & Pay [MOP] platform, which I previously highlighted at 11% penetration, has moved higher to 11.5% of transactions. While the 50 bps may not seem like much, the impact is huge because it is successfully changing consumer behavior by pushing demand to the underutilized walk-up window, which now accounts for ~15% of orders versus 10% previously (2Q25 earnings call). This improvement in throughput and service speed, especially during the morning daypart, provides tangible proof that BROS's digital strategy is driving operational efficiency while enhancing customer experience.

As for the food innovation initiative. What began as a limited test has now doubled in scope to 64 stores and is delivering tangible results. The fact that management expanded the scope tells us that the strategy is working, and indeed, the streamlined eight-item menu has helped to lift both ticket and transaction counts, with management confirming that service times remain largely unaffected. In other words, BROS is able to drive up SSS while maintaining operational efficiency. This effectively de-risks the planned 2026 nationwide rollout, adding another clear driver for future SSS growth.

On another note, I also like to point out that BROS marketing execution has worked out really well. The decision to focus more on paid advertising has helped to drive higher brand awareness and traffic, which is particularly important in growth markets like Texas, where BROS needs to build awareness. The next leg to further drive up awareness is BROS plan to partner with consumer packaged goods [CPG] brands. The way this works is through a licensing partnership with a major manufacturer, for which BROS will roll out branded K-Cups and ground coffee to >50,000 retail outlets in 2026. Per management, this initiative is capital-light and margin-accretive, and if done well, it should serve as a powerful brand marketing tool that helps BROS to extend awareness well beyond its existing store footprint.

Put together, the combination of digital adoption, food innovation, effective marketing, and the upcoming CPG launch are solid growth drivers for SSS.

Valuation

On valuation, relative to June, when the stock traded at 40x forward EBITDA, multiples are now much more palatable at 29x. I am not shifting my P&L assumptions, as what I expect from BROS (in terms of catalyst) is playing out nicely. If anything, I think BROS's visibility to EBITDA growth (driven by revenue growth and margin expansion) has gotten higher. The one thing I have changed is to roll forward my estimates to value on FY27 numbers instead of FY26 (since we are approaching the end of this year). Assuming the same revenue growth and no margin expansion (to be conservative), I get to BROS reporting $395 million in adj. EBITDA. Adjusting for net debt, and you get to a market cap of $13 billion, or a share price of $80. For note, $80 is where BROS traded in February, so it is not far off the historical trading range. This also implies an upside of 34%.

Risk

While I have turned bullish, I think it is also fair to touch more on risk for BROS. While the mid-to-long-term bull case has become clearer, there are near-term issues that readers need to be aware of, which could result in stock price volatility. The first and more concerning one is the tougher comparisons as BROS goes into 2H25. Recall last year that BROS benefited from an outsized growth from the digital rollouts and elevated marketing in late 2024. This has set a high bar, and as those anniversaries come into play, y/y growth rates will likely moderate. The slower headlines may weigh on stock sentiment.

The next issue is on the cost front, in which management has already noted pressure building into 2H25. Beverage, food, and packaging costs are expected to be ~26% of company-operated sales, which means higher coffee prices have yet to flow into the P&L. This is an important detail, as it means gross margin headwinds are still to come, not behind us. Add to this the risk of tariffs on imported goods, which management explicitly called out, and the margin trajectory for 2026 becomes more uncertain. While BROS has demonstrated discipline in scaling sales and maintaining operational efficiency to protect margins, these cost issues are material risks, in my view. Depending on when it hits and how much it hits the P&L, it could temper the stock sentiment as well.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for BROS. The growth flywheel is working well across multiple fronts (digital, food, marketing, and soon CPG), which should drive SSS growth and margin expansion over the mid-to-longer term. With valuation now reset to a far more attractive level and long-term earnings visibility improving, I see a favorable risk/reward profile with ~34% upside potential.

data.title: Ares Capital: Don't Be Fooled By The Dividend Cut Naysayers (Upgrade)
data.description:
Ares Capital: My Caution On The BDC Leader Panned Out As Expected

I hope investors in Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) managed to avoid buying at the recent July highs, as the stock of the leading BDC topped out, heading into its Q2 earnings release then. And it isn't like the company didn't unveil what I thought was still a pretty decent report. If you recall, Ares Capital highlighted higher investment activity in Q2, while also maintaining a relatively low non-accruals ratio (1.2% in fair value terms). Moreover, it also highlighted spillover income that it could depend on to help sustain its dividend allocation in 2025, potentially lowering the market's worries about having to cut its prized dividends.

Despite that, I don't think the market can simply ignore the rate reduction headwinds heading into 2026, particularly as the Fed has finally executed its first interest rate cut for 2025. I held these concerns in my last ARCC write-up, cautioning investors from engaging with the stock too soon, as the risk/reward setup had worsened materially. Hence, I believe my downgrade has proved to be timely, as ARCC has markedly underperformed the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) since then, despite still trading at a relative discount to the broad market.

With the stock having already declined this week, falling to lows last seen in May, I think the market has astutely positioned for a more dovish Fed over the next 12 months. Given the pullback observed in ARCC while the market continues to print new highs, I think we can generally concur that investors don't think the premium observed in the stock is sustainable, as the current interest cycle has definitely peaked and is expected to turn even lower.

Ares Capital: Dealing With Lower Earnings. Dividend Cut Imminent?

Even analysts have penciled in a more challenging prognosis for its earnings capabilities over these two years, notwithstanding the company's best efforts in demonstrating its capital allocation discipline (during the higher interest rate period over the past two years). Yet, I also think it's pivotal to point out that management has been steadfast in telegraphing its confidence that ARCC could maintain its dividend payout, underscoring its 16-year track record without a cut.

Based on the revised Wall Street estimates, analysts expect core EPS of $1.89 to come in below the full dividend payouts of $1.92 for the year, suggesting there could be a need to rely on the spillover income ($878 million, amounting to $1.29 per share), while also being bolstered by the net realized gains of $117 million to help tank any unanticipated decline. Nevertheless, with investment activity ramping up toward the second half of 2025, I believe it should position the company well moving forward, notwithstanding the near-term challenges impeding buying sentiments.

Which is why I think the stock's decline (down 0.33%) was relatively muted following the Fed's interest rate decision yesterday. Based on the updated summary of economic projections, the FOMC's revisions point to two more rate cuts for 2025, and one more for 2026, and 2027, respectively. However, amidst the dovish posture, Fed Chair Powell also took the opportunity to remind us that future decisions will still be data-dependent, and by no means guaranteed.

Given the lack of an imminent economic recession, coupled with Ares Capital's robust backlog as of Q2 (totaling about $2.6 billion), I don't expect the market to go pessimistic on ARCC through 2026. Note that Ares Capital also retains substantive flexibility for ATM equity issuance, while also being bolstered by a relatively solid balance sheet (net leverage ratio of 0.98x and $6.5 billion in liquidity).

Moreover, lower policy rate tailwinds might also help boost originations markedly through 2026, making up for the lower base rates, while also underpinning low accrual rates. Therefore, I believe the astute capital allocation decisions taken when ARCC was then "over-earning," so to speak, should help defend the stock against a sharper selloff through next year.

Buy Or Sell ARCC Stock?

With the decline having panned out since its early 2025 top, ARCC's premium over its financial median has also evaporated. Accordingly, its forward core EPS multiple of 10.6x has dropped below the financial median of 11.2x, while its forward dividend yield of 9.1% has also closed the gap against its 10Y average of 9.5%.

If anything, I think Ares Capital investors thinking about doubling down or increasing exposure might want to take this time to contemplate whether the opportunity to do so is timely.

Based on ARCC's price action assessment, it's evident to me that the stock's uptrend bias is still well-anchored. The Fed's dovish turn has definitely impacted the market's confidence in its capital allocation strategy, but it hasn't been enough to reverse the bullish narrative fully.

And, with the premium in ARCC having already dropped markedly, I think the time to turn more constructive to a healthier 2026 (potentially higher originations and lower default risks) is opportune. Sans an economic recession (which isn't my base case), BDC investors biding their time on the industry leader should find the recent pullback appealing.

Rating: Upgrade to "Buy."

data.title: Blended Finance: Bridging The Sustainability Funding Gap
data.description:
By Patrick O'Connell, CFA and Marie Clara Buellingen

Blended finance has the potential to transform overlooked markets into investable opportunities.

The United Nations (UN) warns of a roughly US$4 trillion annual shortfall in financing for its sustainable development goals—a gap too large for the public sector to fill alone. Blended finance, which combines public, philanthropic and private capital, can help bridge this divide and unlock progress on global priorities such as poverty reduction, climate action and access to clean energy.

A decade after the UN adopted its 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, progress toward many of its goals remains stalled.
